title: An English Friend of Charles of Orléans: Sections B–E
summary: CME00078 is the repository XML for a University of Michigan manually keyed and proofread electronic text derived from Henry Noble MacCracken's 'An English Friend of Charles of Orleans,' PMLA 26.1 (1911), but only sections B–E on printed pp. 155–180. Section B transcribes the twenty anonymous poems of the Fairfax Sequence from Bodleian MS Fairfax 16, ff. 318r–329r. Section C reprints nine English poems from Champollion-Figeac's 1842 edition, pp. 265–270: modern item-level evidence and direct image inspection locate them in BnF français 25458, pp. 310–313 and 346, while Champollion names Le Roux de Lincy's working copies and other French manuscript sources; MacCracken's simple 'Grenoble' label is therefore not treated as a direct witness claim. Section D reprints Champollion pp. 455–456, whose note identifies Jules Delpit's copies from Royal MS 16 F II; the two poems occur at ff. 118r and 131r. Section E transcribes one poem from Arundel MS 26, f. 32v, and one from the Findern manuscript, historically f. 178r and now f. 154r. A 2025 article reports an earlier collateral witness to the Arundel poem at TNA C 47/34/1/4. Complete public scans of the PMLA issue, Champollion edition, Fairfax 16, BnF français 25458, Grenoble U.1091, and Findern Ff.1.6 are cached, with target portions mapped inside each complete object where overlap is established. British Library and TNA witnesses remain requestable or unavailable rather than locally cached.
review_notes: The cataloged unit is an edited selection across four source sections, not one medieval codex, anthology, or securely single-authored work.
MacCracken's William de la Pole attribution is recorded as a disputed historical claim; current Bodleian cataloging describes the Fairfax Sequence as anonymous.

Champollion's preface and note 73 distinguish his Grenoble base from additional English pieces copied by Le Roux de Lincy. Section C is therefore not modeled as a direct transcription of Grenoble U.1091.
The nine BnF section C poems are discontiguous: seven occupy manuscript pp. 310–313 and two occupy p. 346.
Champollion explicitly says Jules Delpit copied the Royal poems and sent them to Paris, so the working copy is retained as an intermediary rather than silently collapsed.

XML page breaks run consecutively from printed p. 155 through p. 180. Section C begins on p. 174, D on p. 178, and E on p. 179 after earlier sections on the same page, so no duplicate PB element is expected at those section boundaries.
The complete Grenoble U.1091 bundle is retained as source-evaluation evidence, but no target-poem overlap is asserted.
Complete-manuscript caches represent whole published digital objects; work_portion locators associate the target without cropping the downloaded source.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00078
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID are all CME00078.
The XML records revision on 2016-09-16 and says a French fragment was removed from the beginning.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item CME00078
entities.description: A public manually keyed and proofread electronic text encoding MacCracken's sections B–E, their headings, notes, verse groups, printed page breaks, and manuscript milestones.

entities.label: Henry Noble MacCracken, An English Friend of Charles of Orleans (1911)
entities.description: The immediate source article. CME00078 selects sections B–E on pp. 155–180 and omits section A on pp. 142–155. MacCracken supplies manuscript-based transcriptions, source notes, cross-comparisons, and an argument for William de la Pole's authorship.

bibliographic.citation: Henry Noble MacCracken, 'An English Friend of Charles of Orleans,' Publications of the Modern Language Association of America 26.1 (1911), 142–180.
bibliographic.title: An English Friend of Charles of Orleans
bibliographic.container_title: Publications of the Modern Language Association of America

notes: Section B begins partway down p. 155; C begins on p. 174, D on p. 178, and E on p. 179. The article and encoded selection end on p. 180.

entities.label: Aimé Champollion-Figeac, Les poésies du duc Charles d'Orléans (1842)
entities.description: An edition based principally on the Grenoble manuscript and compared with manuscripts in Paris and London. MacCracken reprints section C from pp. 265–270 and section D from pp. 455–456. Champollion's notes identify Le Roux de Lincy's copies of English pieces and Jules Delpit's copies from Royal 16 F II.

bibliographic.citation: Aimé Champollion-Figeac, ed., Les poésies du duc Charles d'Orléans publiées sur le manuscrit de la Bibliothèque de Grenoble, conféré avec ceux de Paris et de Londres (Paris: J. Belin-Leprieur et Colomb de Batines, 1842).
bibliographic.title: Les poésies du duc Charles d'Orléans
bibliographic.publisher: J. Belin-Leprieur et Colomb de Batines

entities.label: Le Roux de Lincy's working copies of English poems for Champollion
entities.description: A provisionally modeled documentary intermediary: Champollion's note 73 says Antoine Le Roux de Lincy copied the English pieces for him. No surviving standalone transcript was identified in this research.
notes: The schema has no dedicated working-transcript entity type; catalog_description is used provisionally without implying that a modern catalog record survives.

entities.label: Jules Delpit's working copies from Royal MS 16 F II
entities.description: A provisionally modeled documentary intermediary: Champollion states that Jules Delpit copied the English pieces from Royal MS 16 F II and sent them to Paris.
notes: The exact form and present survival of Delpit's copy have not been established.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Fairfax 16
entities.description: A mid-fifteenth-century parchment miscellany. The anonymous twenty-poem Fairfax Sequence occupies ff. 318r–329r and is MacCracken's section B source.

date_statements.label: current catalog date
date_statements.value: likely written in the 1440s, possibly completed 1450

holding.notes: The current catalog identifies the Fairfax Sequence as anonymous and gives exact folios.

physical_description.extent: ff. i + 340 + i; current complete digital object also includes binding surfaces
notes: The complete digital object has 721 canvases. The target begins on f. 318r and ends on f. 329r; XML's first explicit folio milestone is 318v because the opening recto is implicit.

entities.label: Paris, Bibliothèque nationale de France, Français 25458
entities.description: Charles d'Orléans's complex personal poetry manuscript, containing English pieces among predominantly French material. All nine section C incipits were visually confirmed on manuscript pp. 310–313 and 346.

date_statements.label: principal production period
date_statements.notes: The date of the complete manuscript does not by itself date every English addition.

holding.notes: The Gallica object supplies a complete 606-canvas digital surrogate.

physical_description.support: paper and parchment components described in the official record
physical_description.extent: 537 numbered pages plus inserted blanks and end material
notes: The target is discontiguous: seven poems on pp. 310–313 and two poems on p. 346.

entities.label: Grenoble, Bibliothèque municipale, U. 1091 Rés.
entities.description: A complete Charles d'Orléans poetry manuscript digitized by Pagella. It was Champollion's principal stated manuscript, but no target-poem overlap for CME00078 section C has been established; it is retained as source-evaluation evidence rather than a direct ancestor of the English selection.

holding.notes: Legacy catalog number 873; the complete IIIF object has 330 canvases.

entities.label: London, British Library, Royal MS 16 F II
entities.description: A late-fifteenth-century parchment manuscript containing the two section D English poems at ff. 118r and 131r. Legacy full digital coverage is recorded but was unavailable when checked after the British Library cyberattack.

holding.notes: Use the current record for physical consultation and reproduction enquiry.

notes: The target poems begin 'My hertly love is in your governauns' at f. 118r and 'Ne were my trewe innocent hert' at f. 131r.

entities.label: London, British Library, Arundel MS 26
entities.description: A sixteenth-century paper miscellany containing 'A Balade colourd and reversid,' beginning 'Honnour and beaute, vertue and gentilnesse,' at f. 32v. MacCracken prints it as section E, poem I.

holding.notes: No complete public digital surrogate was found.

entities.label: Cambridge, University Library, MS Ff.1.6 (Findern manuscript)
entities.description: A defective fifteenth- to sixteenth-century paper household anthology associated with Findern, Derbyshire. MacCracken's section E poem II occupies historical f. 178r, mapped by direct visual comparison to current CUDL f. 154r and IIIF canvas 311.

holding.notes: Current CUDL TEI and IIIF identifiers are recorded in the access entry.

notes: The manuscript is defective, with 29 leaves reported missing. The complete current digital object has 329 canvases.

entities.label: Kew, The National Archives, C 47/34/1/4
entities.description: A newly reported mid-fifteenth-century copy of 'A Balade colourd and reversid' on a reused oblong paper strip in the Chancery Miscellanea. It is an earlier collateral witness to section E poem I, not a source MacCracken used.

holding.notes: The exact piece-level public catalog page was not identified. The public C 47 browse record is a parent-series route rather than an exact item record; request by the full reference.

notes: Sobecki describes an informal trained mid-fifteenth-century Anglicana hand.

entities.description: Complete 235-page issue scan containing MacCracken's article and its full bibliographic context.
entities.reproduction_format: PDF page images with OCR layer

entities.description: Complete published 329-canvas digital object of the surviving manuscript, including cover, endleaves, all surviving leaves, back cover, and spine.

scope.notes: The article's section A, pp. 142–155, is not encoded.

assertion.notes: MacCracken cites the manuscript directly; whether he personally inspected it or used an unreported copy remains unverified.

Canvas numbers 649–671 are one-based positions in the cached provider inventory, not zero-based array indices.

work_portion.label: Nine section C English poems
notes: The target is discontiguous; start and end links delimit but do not imply continuous target coverage.
The complete object is cached at the requested 1800-pixel width profile where supported.
Provider canvas identifiers f358–f361 and f394 coincide with one-based inventory positions 358–361 and 394 in this object; they are not stated as zero-based indices.

local_copies.label: Complete Grenoble U.1091 Rés. IIIF bundle

work_portion.label: No confirmed CME00078 target overlap
locators: Complete manuscript retained for source evaluation
notes: No section C work portion is asserted; the complete bundle documents why MacCracken's broad Grenoble label needs qualification.
Cached from the complete official object at a requested 1800-pixel width profile.

local_copies.label: Complete Cambridge MS Ff.1.6 Findern IIIF bundle

work_portion.label: A mercy, Fortune, have pitee on me
notes: Historical and current foliation were reconciled by visual comparison; current f. 154v / canvas 312 is blank.
Canvas 311 is a one-based position in the cached provider inventory, not a zero-based array index.

CUDL's 328 requested 1800-width deliveries were constrained by a 2000-pixel height cap and are approximately 1140–1320 pixels wide; target f. 154r is 1146 × 2000 pixels. One canvas used a 512 × 2000 native fallback. The bundle is therefore complete but not a uniform 1800-pixel-width derivative.
The record reports full legacy digital coverage, but the old viewer and inferred replacement image endpoint were unavailable when checked.

access.access_method: Request a complete color reproduction of Royal MS 16 F II, including the binding, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, and all ff. i + 248; identify target ff. 118r and 131r separately without limiting the whole-object request. Alternatively consult the object in a British Library reading room.
notes: A quotation is required; no complete-object price has been obtained.

access.access_method: Request a complete color reproduction of Arundel MS 26, including the binding, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, and all 75 folios; identify target f. 32v separately without limiting the whole-object request. Alternatively consult the object in a British Library reading room.
notes: A quotation is required; no complete-object price has been obtained.

access.access_method: Request a complete color reproduction of C 47/34/1/4 and its relevant enclosure or physical context, citing the exact reference and Sobecki's 2025 article.
access.format: Repository-supplied digital copy, subject to condition and quotation
notes: The initial page check starts at £9.92, after which TNA supplies a copy quotation; whole or high-volume requests are handled separately.

rights.component: Electronic text as distributed by the University of Michigan CME collection
rights.basis: The current U-M CME collection applies No Copyright–United States to the public-domain source editions and collection materials.
notes: This does not determine rights in manuscript photographs supplied by other institutions.

rights.basis: Published in the United States in 1911; the printed article is in the U.S. public domain. Internet Archive access terms and scan metadata remain separate layers.

rights.jurisdiction: United States and source jurisdiction as represented by Wikimedia Commons
rights.basis: The 1842 edition is public domain by age; the Commons file page marks the digital reproduction with the Public Domain Mark.
The official IIIF attribution states © Bodleian Libraries and links CC BY-NC 4.0.
notes: Commercial reuse, including use connected to print-on-demand sales, is outside the stated noncommercial license unless separately permitted.

rights.basis: The current Gallica conditions permit noncommercial reuse free of charge with required source attribution, treat commercial reuse as licensed and normally paid, and state an exemption for scientific or academic publication. They are provider reuse terms, not a Creative Commons license.
notes: Git-hosted reference access must satisfy the noncommercial and attribution conditions; print-on-demand or other commercial reuse requires BnF licensing unless the provider confirms an applicable exemption.
Gallica states that documents held by partner institutions may remain subject to partner terms; this record concerns a BnF-held object.

rights.basis: The official Pagella manifest attributes digitization to the BnF and embeds a Pagella-hosted Gallica-conditions URL that is now unavailable. The live Gallica terms say partner-held documents may be subject to the partner institution's conditions, so the exact Grenoble reuse grant remains unverified.
notes: Do not treat the BnF-hosted general terms as a confirmed open license for this Grenoble-held partner object; obtain written terms from Bibliothèque municipale de Grenoble before redistribution or commercial use.

rights.component: Cambridge Digital Library downloadable manuscript images
rights.basis: The official IIIF attribution states that downloadable images and metadata are licensed CC BY-NC 3.0, while zooming images are all rights reserved; a displayed watermark may mention CC BY-NC 4.0, so the more specific manifest terms are retained with the discrepancy noted.
notes: Commercial reuse requires separate permission; distinguish downloadable images from zoom-only delivery layers.

rights.component: Any British Library supplied manuscript reproductions for Royal MS 16 F II
rights.basis: No image delivery or exact license has been obtained; public-domain status of medieval text does not determine reproduction-service terms or image rights.

rights.component: Any British Library supplied manuscript reproductions for Arundel MS 26
rights.basis: No image delivery or exact license has been obtained; public-domain status of medieval text does not determine reproduction-service terms or image rights.

No copy or delivery license has been obtained. TNA's record-copying terms govern supplied copies, restrict further reproduction without permission, and must be reviewed with any quotation or delivery.

editorial_practices.description: MacCracken assembles four groups from distinct manuscript and printed-source routes to support a historical authorship argument; the grouping is editorial rather than a medieval collection boundary.
editorial_practices.representation: Four numbered or lettered DIV1 sections in CME00078

editorial_practices.description: The electronic text begins with MacCracken's section B and ends with section E; section A and the article opening are omitted.
editorial_practices.representation: Complete encoding of the selected four sections, including consecutive PB milestones 155–180

editorial_practices.description: MacCracken cross-references parallel poems and variants across the Fairfax, Charles d'Orléans-associated, Royal, Arundel, and Findern materials, but the precise indirect source chain for sections C and D must be retained.
editorial_practices.representation: Editorial notes and cross-references retained in the XML

open_questions.question: What exact manuscript and working-copy chain underlies each section C poem, given MacCracken's 'Grenoble' label, Champollion's references to La Vallière, Carpentras, and Le Roux de Lincy's copies, and modern confirmation in BnF français 25458?
next_steps: Collate all nine Champollion readings against BnF français 25458, Grenoble U.1091, and the relevant Carpentras manuscript.
Locate surviving Le Roux de Lincy or Champollion papers and determine whether one or several working transcripts intervened.
Replace the provisional catalog_description intermediary if the actual transcript survives and can be described precisely.

open_questions.question: Which individual poems can current scholarship securely assign to William de la Pole, Charles d'Orléans, or another author, and which remain anonymous?
next_steps: Build a poem-level concordance to modern JONAS, DIMEV, Fox–Arn, Pearsall, and Neilly records.
Keep MacCracken's common Suffolk attribution historical until individual assignments are supported.

open_questions.question: Can the British Library restore or supply complete color digital facsimiles of Royal MS 16 F II and Arundel MS 26, and what exact storage, Git-publication, viewer, print-on-demand, and commercial permissions would govern them?
next_steps: Ask whether the legacy full Royal digital object will return and request its stable IIIF or download URL.

If ordering, request each complete present physical object, including covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every leaf, edges, and detached matter where offered; identify target folios separately.
Retain quotations, delivery inventories, correspondence, and exact licenses with any supplied files.

open_questions.question: Can TNA supply a complete color reproduction of C 47/34/1/4 with enough enclosure and physical context to document the reused strip, and what reuse terms would apply?
next_steps: Request the exact item by full reference and cite Sobecki 2025.
Ask for recto, verso, scale or color target, envelope or enclosure context, and any catalog metadata not visible online.
Record the delivery license before any repository publication.

open_questions.question: Do the BnF, Pagella, CUDL, and Bodleian terms permit the intended Git-hosted reference access and any downstream print-on-demand or commercial use of their manuscript images?
next_steps: Treat CC BY-NC images as noncommercial unless separate permission is granted.
Ask BnF and Grenoble for written permission or a commercial license if images themselves would be redistributed in a commercial workflow.
Keep underlying medieval text rights distinct from rights or contract terms governing modern photographs and metadata.
